speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 10 of Barbara Traub's interview. So Barbara will you tell us who's surrounding you right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: At my left is my husband, Herman. And at my right is my son, Ralph--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --presently, the two men in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what made this meeting on the steps of the New School different than the ones before? What made this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: For him or for me?

speaker: interviewer
text: For you, Barbara.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me-- I didn't know him because it was a blind date. So I came out, and I was going down the street. And I suddenly heard a voice in Hungarian saying [HUNGARIAN], which means, what would you like to do tonight?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I turned around and I said, are you Herbert Traub? Because I thought his name was Herbert. And he said, yes. And I guess that was one of those things, that he took the trouble to learn a Hungarian phrase.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one was, frankly, that after we had drinks at Peter's Backyard, which doesn't exist anymore, and he took me home, he very respectfully said goodnight to me at the door-- at the front door, not before my apartment. Didn't insist for a nightcap, things which I disliked very much when I had to argue with my dates, that I don't want-- I'm not prepared for nightcaps. So I, I, I thought that was one of the things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, the third thing was that while we were speaking over the telephone arranging for this blind date, he made a reference to Arthur Schnitzler, who is one of the playwrights. And I asked him, are you an American? And he said yes, and that was very surprising because very few Americans knew of Arthur Schnitzler. So this is what I found. Well, I'm taking the chance. Did it work out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here we are, 38 years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to show you what kind of an effect Barbara had upon me at the very outset, I deliberately was very careful when I took her home. And since I knew that this was going to be a long range relationship, and I felt already serious about it, I just said good-bye at the front door. And, and I knew things would progress from there on. And they did. And I'm happy to say that it all worked out very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about her did you just know? How did you know that it was going to be a long range plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's always one of the mysteries of life where you get a feeling that this is going to work out. There were a lot of very attractive things, as far as I was concerned, about Baba, and including her looks, to start with. And then her European background, which was very attractive to me because I had spent some time in Europe, and I was European oriented. My father was a European.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I felt very comfortable with Baba right off-- right off the bat. And our relationship deepened and finally got to the point of marriage. And how about naming your son after your father? Is there-- do you see similarities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his tall like my father was. But, again, I was a good girl, because his second name is Ralph, and that is after Herman's father. His first name is Nahum. And although I grew up with the tradition that the first born son's name is given by the wife, whatever name she chooses. I mean, I don't know whether this is a written law. But, certainly, this is what I remembered from the customs in our Jewish society in the old country. And I knew that I could have any name I wanted. And, clearly, I wanted my father's name. But I also wanted to please my husband, who lost his father in 1945. And I did it for him. And so I said, his Hebrew will be Nahoum, and his secular name will be after his other grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've said that his bar mitzvah was one of the happiest days of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes it was. Yes it was. And the way he cantilated his Haftorah was one of the most beautiful musics. You asked me earlier about why the thirst for knowledge, and I spoke in terms of something that moves your soul. Well this did move my soul. It was just magnificent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it mean a lot to you that it meant a lot to your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you see qualities in your mom that makes you think-- makes you understand why she would survive as opposed to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can answer that, I think. The best way of, of expressing it is a Hebrew expression, [HEBREW], a woman of valor. After having known Baba for 39 years, 38 of which are in marriage, I, I am confident in describing her as a woman of valor. And it was not just a question of her having survived. But more than that-- although that's an important element-- there was a real question of productivity, doing something with her life, which she did in many respects. And was able-- while the Auschwitz was always there, and never quite abated, simultaneously, she was able to function extremely well as a wife, as a mother, as an author, as a person who was involved in community affairs. And then when asked, to speak on the Holocaust. So that there's been a very useful core, very great productivity, in her life. And I recognized that quality at the very outset.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Anybody have anything else today? Do his kids--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to add that I grew up with my father, fully. And that my mother was, and is, my best friend. And I love her dearly

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything you want to tell your grandkids about your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No secrets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They shouldn't talk as much as your mom does?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said sotto voce to my son, who always feels that I am rather generous in my talk. Rather verbal, so perhaps he would want his grandchildren to be a little less voluminous than I am. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you want to say? One last thing to his grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like his grandchildren to know is that, That knowledge is the only thing that nobody can take away from you. As that lady said to me. I think that being Jewish has a lot to do with being who we are. And that goes not only for those who are Jewish-- who are Jews-- but to all those people who belong to ethnic groups, whether they are-- whatever they are. That I would like my grandchildren to be what they are, and be good at it and be proud at it-- about it. And be good people without bias, without prejudice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And thank you, Steven Spielberg, for making this possible. This is my mother when she married my father, which must have been in the beginning of the 20th century. She must have been about 17, judging by her dress. And the picture, I'm sure, was taken in Sziget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Regina Fischman, born Stauber, S-T-A-U-B-E-R. And her Hebrew name was Rivka. Wait a minute, let me try to figure out all my secrets. This is a picture of my father, the way I remember him in the late '20s, with the mustache. And he shaved his mustache, because when he was kissing me and hugging me it was scratching my face. And eventually he shaved his mustache. And that's an early picture of my father. It was also taken in Sziget. The name is Nandor, the Hungarian name, Ferdinand, the Romanian name, Fischman. This is a picture of the class of the kindergarten kids I attended. It was probably in 1929 in Sziget. The two center adult women were our teachers. The one to the left is the main teacher. The second one-- the younger one-- was an assistant teacher. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the little girl in the middle is a picture of me. This is a picture of me at the age of 9 or 10. It's a rather formal picture, done by the utmost photo-- I mean, the well-known photographer in the town of Sziget, by the name of Sabo. And it must have been 1933, '34, perhaps. I remember it was a green dress with a white pique bib, or trimming. And I had long hair and black taffeta bows in my hair. This is a picture of me at the age of 11 or 12 after my mother took me to have my hair cut because I was of growing up. And I was dressed the way a quote, nice, unquote, girl should wear-- should be dressed with the white gloves and the little pocketbook-- retikul it was called-- and, of course, knee socks. And that was, I suppose, the attire that quote nice girls should wear. This is a picture taken on a Saturday morning, on the Shabbat morning when the family was coming back from the synagogue. The picture is taken in around 1938. And I will start with my mother who is on the left. She was still in mourning after my sister's death. And under her left arm is the siddur, the prayer book that she used in the synagogue every Shabbat morning. Next to her is my older brother, Mano, Moshe, also coming from the synagogue. The family always went together. And next to him is me. That very Saturday morning on the corso in Sziget when all the Jewish shops were closed because it was Shabbat morning. This is, again, a picture of me walking arm in arm with my father. It was not a Shabbat morning because he doesn't have a hat. It was just an ordinary day walking through the corso with my father. And looking at the picture, I know how happy I was and how good life was when I walked arm in arm with my father. This is I, age 13, wearing the uniform-- the black dress with the white pique collar and the polka dot bow-- in the backyard of my parents' house. This is a picture of myself and a friend skiing in the Carpathian Mountains, which were right-- not far from my town. We could walk it, actually. We could walk up to the skiing place. And I was about 15 years old. And you can see the very old fashioned skis with old fashioned bindings-- long, wooden skis. And it took us a whole day to get to the top of the mountains. They didn't have ski lifts or anything. And it took us another half a day-- rather, half a day to get up to the mountain, and a half a day to come back-- to come down. The friend I am with did not survive. He perished in Auschwitz. This is a picture of my mother and one of her grandchildren, a little boy by the name of Andicka, about a year or so old. He was the child of my brother's, Mano, Moshe. And my mother was so happy with this baby, as she was with the other grandchildren, except that when this picture was taken the child's father was still in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And neither mother nor grandchild survived. This is my little niece, named Anika, age two, two and a half, my other brother's little girl who did not survive either. This is my brother Alex, [? Shonny, ?] 1948 in the Haganah. He's the father of the little girl, Anika, that we saw before. And he fought in the war of independence-- of Israel's war of independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives, now, in Westbury, Long Island. This is Leonid Mikhailovich Geller, 1945, July or August, the man-- the Russian Jewish officer who helped me cry, weep, for the first time after Auschwitz. The date is June 9, 1957. The wedding date between Herman-- the wedding day of Herman and my wedding day. Cutting the cake during our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the wedding was in New York at the Shery-Netherland's Hotel, Fifth Avenue. And I believe it doesn't exist anymore. But it was between 59 and 60th street and 5th. The specialty of this picture is not that I'm chairing this dinner dance for Hadassah Medical Organization, which raised that night millions of dollars. It was in November, the year that Arafat received permit to come to America for the first time. The dinner dance was, was scheduled to take place at the Waldorf Astoria. And Arafat was housed at the Waldorf Astoria, and he arrived two days before the date of this dinner dance. I refused to conduct-- to chair a dinner at the Waldorf Astoria while Arafat was also at the Waldorf Astoria. I got full support from my coworkers, and overnight we switched that dinner dance to the Hilton. And this is the picture of that. And there is, among others, Chaim Gross, this second from-- or rather, the second from the left, Mrs. Gross and Chaim Gross, and other dignitaries, including Rusk-- Professor Rusk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure which one these are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prof--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which to focus on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Professor Rusk is the second person--

speaker: interviewer
text: On this side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- from my right side. He wears a tuxedo. And that evening we anticipated to raise about $1 million for Hadassah, but we raised three times as much because everybody supported and applauded the decision not to have the dinner dance at the Waldorf Astoria because of Arafat. Month of June-- end of June, actually-- 1976. My son's bar mitzvah. The picture says it all. This is the, the cover of my historical novel, called published in 1979. The subject of it is the return of a group of survivors from a death camp to their home town in [? olden ?] Transylvania. Clearly, it is about Sziget. And, to a great extent, the novel is biographical-- autobiographical. This picture was taken in Jerusalem, 1982, during the world meeting of Jewish Partisan resistance fighters. I was there because I was doing a book on Jewish resistance. And I am flanked by the two Bielski brothers. There were four brothers, legendary heroes, who maintained a camp under the nose of the Nazis and also carried out many Partisan and raids against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: April 7, 1994. That is just about a year ago at the Vatican, during the commemoration of the Shoah. And this is during the private audience that the 100 survivors had with the pope. And he listened to what I had to say about being victimized twice if the first victimization is not acknowledged. And this is the only heirloom, the only legacy, that I can touch that I have from my mother's house. She made this handarbeit, this, this work. I remember her crocheting it. And I found it, and it's all I have. And when I touch it, I think I feel my mother. I use it once a year in the center, and I put it over the white tablecloth in the center of our Seder table. And I hope that my grandchildren will continue doing that.
